meta.name: ESSI ENRICHD Social Support Instrument
meta.description: The ESSI is a brief 6-item self-report measure of perceived social support. It assesses the availability of emotional support, informational support, instrumental support, and affectionate support. Originally developed for cardiac patients in the ENRICHD trial, it is widely used across clinical and community populations.
meta.author: Mitchell et al. (2003)
meta.reference: Mitchell PH, Powell L, Blumenthal J, et al. A short social support measure for patients recovering from myocardial infarction: The ENRICHD Social Support Instrument. J Cardiopulm Rehabil. 2003;23(6):398-403.
meta.instruction: Please read each statement carefully and indicate how much of the time each statement is true for you.

questions.text: Is there someone available to you whom you can count on to listen to you when you need to talk?
Is there someone available to give you good advice about a problem?
Is there someone available to you who shows you love and affection?
Is there someone available to help you with daily chores?
Can you count on anyone to provide you with emotional support (talking over problems or helping you make a difficult decision)?
Do you have as much contact as you would like with someone you feel close to, someone in whom you can trust and confide?

ranges.label: Low Social Support
ranges.description: Your score indicates a low level of perceived social support. You may feel that few people are available to help or support you emotionally. Your mental health concerns are notable and may be impacting daily functioning. Speaking with a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Consider reaching out to community organizations, support groups, or religious communities
Try reconnecting with old friends or family members
Volunteer or join clubs to expand your social network
Consider talking to a counselor about building social connections
Online communities can also provide valuable support

ranges.label: Moderate Social Support
ranges.description: Your score indicates a moderate level of social support. You have some support available but may feel it is not always sufficient. At this level, psychological concerns may be affecting your daily life. It is recommended to take active steps and consider available support options.
suggestions: Identify areas where you need more support and communicate your needs
Strengthen existing relationships through regular contact
Explore joining interest-based groups or activities
Practice asking for help when you need it
Consider couples or family counseling to improve close relationships

ranges.label: High Social Support
ranges.description: Your score indicates a high level of social support. You generally feel well-supported by those around you. Mild psychological concerns are common in life. Focusing on self-care and healthy coping strategies can help maintain well-being.
suggestions: Continue nurturing your supportive relationships
Express gratitude to those who support you
Consider ways to reciprocate the support you receive
Maintain regular social contact to keep connections strong
Focus on self-care with good routines and regular exercise

ranges.label: Very High Social Support
ranges.description: Your score indicates a very high level of perceived social support. You feel strongly supported in multiple areas of your life. This indicates that your overall mental health is in good condition, with all areas of functioning working well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your strong social network
Share your social support strategies with others who may be more isolated
Use your support network as a resource during challenging times
Consider mentoring or supporting others in need
Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle and mental well-being
